---
title: "Hypothesis Testing — The 7-Step Framework"
categories: [statistics, procedure, inference]
tier-focus: procedure
filters:
  - webr
related:
  - ../../wiki/concepts/hypothesis-testing-overview.md
  - ../../wiki/concepts/which-test.md
  - ../../wiki/r-code/hypothesis-tests-r.md
---

# Hypothesis Testing — The 7-Step Framework

## Learning Goals

1. Apply the 7-step hypothesis testing procedure to any test
2. Distinguish Type I and Type II errors and explain their consequences
3. Interpret p-values using the teacher's expected phrasing
4. Compute one-sample Z and t tests in R

## In Plain English

Hypothesis testing is a formal decision procedure. You assume nothing is happening (H₀), then ask: "How unlikely is my data if H₀ were true?" If very unlikely (p-value < α), you reject H₀.

You never **prove** H₀ is true — you only fail to find evidence against it.

## The 7-Step Procedure

| Step | Action |
|------|--------|
| 1 | Identify the **parameter of interest** (μ, σ², p) |
| 2 | State **H₀** (null hypothesis — "no effect") |
| 3 | State **H₁** (alternative — two-sided ≠, upper >, lower <) |
| 4 | Choose the **test statistic** formula |
| 5 | State the **rejection criterion** (critical value or p-value < α) |
| 6 | **Compute** the test statistic and p-value |
| 7 | **Conclude** in plain language, in context |

## Error Types

| | H₀ True | H₀ False |
|---|---------|---------|
| **Fail to reject H₀** | Correct ✓ | Type II error (β) |
| **Reject H₀** | Type I error (α) | Correct ✓ (Power = 1−β) |

## Teacher's Conclusion Phrasing

**Reject H₀:**
> "Because p-value = [value] < α = [value], we reject H₀. There is sufficient evidence to conclude that [H₁ in context] at α = [value]."

**Fail to reject H₀:**
> "Because p-value = [value] > α = [value], we fail to reject H₀. There is insufficient evidence to conclude that [H₁ in context] at α = [value]."

## R Example: One-Sample t-Test

```{webr-r}
#| label: one-sample-t

# ── Change these to explore ──────────────────────────
rod_lengths <- c(9.8, 10.2, 9.7, 10.5, 10.1, 9.9, 10.3, 9.6, 10.4, 10.0)
mu0         <- 10           # null hypothesis value  (try 10.1, 9.9)
alternative <- "two.sided"  # "two.sided", "greater", "less"
alpha       <- 0.05         # significance level     (try 0.01)
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

result <- t.test(rod_lengths, mu = mu0, alternative = alternative,
                 conf.level = 1 - alpha)
print(result)

cat("\nt* =", round(result$statistic, 4), "\n")
cat("p-value =", round(result$p.value, 4), "\n")
cat("df =", result$parameter, "\n")

direction <- switch(alternative,
  two.sided = "≠", greater = ">", less = "<")
if (result$p.value < alpha) {
  cat(sprintf("\nDecision: Reject H₀. Sufficient evidence that μ %s %.1f at α = %.2f.\n",
              direction, mu0, alpha))
} else {
  cat(sprintf("\nDecision: Fail to reject H₀. Insufficient evidence that μ %s %.1f at α = %.2f.\n",
              direction, mu0, alpha))
}
```

## Try It Yourself

Run the interactive block above and verify these results:

1. **One-sided test:** Change `alternative` to `"greater"`. How does the p-value change? Why does a one-sided test have more power?
2. **Null shift:** Change `mu0` to `10.1`. Does the conclusion change? How close to the sample mean does the null have to be before you fail to reject?
3. **Significance level:** Change `alpha` to `0.01`. Does the same evidence lead to the same decision? What Type I error consequence does that tighter threshold represent in this context?

## Related Wiki Pages

- [Hypothesis Testing Overview](../../wiki/concepts/hypothesis-testing-overview.md)
- [Which Test?](../../wiki/concepts/which-test.md)
- [R: Hypothesis Tests](../../wiki/r-code/hypothesis-tests-r.md)
- [Power and Sample Size](../../wiki/concepts/power-sample-size.md)
